name: Girdle of Aphrodite
description: The Girdle of Aphrodite, also known as the Cestus, is a magical belt or sash possessed by the Greek goddess of love, beauty, pleasure, and procreation, Aphrodite. It is described as being woven with artistry and imbued with the power to incite irresistible love, desire, and attraction. The girdle held all of Aphrodite's charm and allure, and when worn, it made the wearer utterly captivating to anyone who beheld them. It was not merely an object of beauty; it was a potent instrument of influence and manipulation, capable of swaying even the most stalwart hearts. The girdle was often lent to other goddesses and even mortals to enhance their own appeal and secure romantic or strategic advantages. Hera, the queen of the gods, famously borrowed the girdle to seduce Zeus and distract him from the Trojan War. The girdle's power was so profound that it could even affect animals, causing them to succumb to the wearer's charm. Its significance lies in its embodiment of the raw, untamed power of love and desire, and its ability to shape the course of events through the manipulation of human emotions. The girdle represents the seductive and persuasive nature of Aphrodite's domain, a reminder that love and beauty can be powerful forces in the world.
subtitle: A belt of irresistible allure.
origin: Created by Aphrodite or bestowed upon her

keyMyths.title: Hera's Deception
keyMyths.description: Hera borrows Aphrodite's girdle to seduce Zeus, distracting him from the Trojan War and aiding the Achaeans. This highlights the girdle's power to manipulate even the king of the gods.

keyMyths.title: Impact on Mortals
keyMyths.description: While not directly mentioned in specific myths concerning heroes, the girdle's power to influence attraction would have invariably affected mortal relationships and desires. Its influence is widespread.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Describes Hera borrowing the girdle to seduce Zeus.
sources.text: Describes Hera borrowing the girdle to seduce Zeus.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides context for Aphrodite's powers and domain.
